title: Cell-Based Architecture — Blast Radius Containment at Planet Scale
subtitle: How FAANG isolates failures and limits impact by sharding infrastructure into independent cells — and what SAP can learn for multi-tenant SaaS

sections.content:
## The Problem Cell-Based Architecture Solves

In a traditional monolithic or even microservices architecture, a single failure can cascade across the entire system:

- A bad deployment in one service corrupts data for all tenants
- A traffic spike in one region overwhelms shared infrastructure used by all regions
- A storage backend failure takes down every shard that depends on it
- A misconfigured change propagates instantly to every instance

**Cell-Based Architecture (CBA)** solves this by partitioning infrastructure into independent 'cells' — each cell is a complete, self-contained instance of the entire service stack. A failure in one cell stays in that cell. The blast radius is contained.

### Core Principle

> A cell is a **vertically integrated, horizontally sharded** unit of infrastructure that can operate independently of every other cell.

Each cell contains its own:
- Compute (Kubernetes cluster, Auto Scaling Group)
- Storage (database, cache, object store)
- Networking (load balancer, DNS, TLS certs)
- Configuration (feature flags, routing rules)
- Monitoring (metrics, logs, alerts)

Cells are connected only through a thin 'super-router' that routes requests to the correct cell based on tenant ID, region, or request attributes.

cards.title: Cell vs Microservices
cards.body:
**Microservices** partition by function (auth service, payment service, etc.). Each service is independent, but they share infrastructure (Kubernetes cluster, database). A failure in one service can cascade.

**Cell-Based Architecture** partitions by tenant/region. Each cell is a complete, independent deployment of ALL services for a subset of tenants. A failure in Cell A doesn't affect Cell B.

**Key difference:** Microservices isolate *function*. Cells isolate *tenants*.

**Can they combine?** Yes. Each cell can internally use microservices. But the cells themselves are the primary isolation boundary.

cards.title: Cell vs Sharding
cards.body:
**Sharding** partitions *data* across multiple databases. All shards share the same application code.

**Cell-Based Architecture** partitions *everything* — data, compute, networking, monitoring.

**Key difference:** A shard failure takes down one slice of the data but the application layer still works. A cell failure takes down everything for that cell's tenants, but other cells are completely unaffected.

**When to use each:** Sharding for database scaling. CBA for failure isolation. They can coexist (each cell contains multiple shards).

cards.title: Blast Radius — The Key Metric
cards.body:
**Blast radius** = the percentage of users impacted by a single failure.

In a monolithic system: Blast radius = 100% (one failure takes down everything).

In a microservices system: Blast radius = 10-50% (a critical service failure can cascade).

In a cell-based system: Blast radius = 1/N (where N is the number of cells).

**Example:** 100 cells, each serving 1% of users. A cell failure impacts 1% of users. The remaining 99% continue unaffected.

**Staff+ metric:** Blast radius is the single most important resilience metric. CBA reduces it from 100% to 1/N.

cards.title: Cell Topologies
cards.body:
**Three common cell topologies:**

**1. Regional cells** — Each region is a cell. Users are routed to the closest cell. Latency-optimized, but a regional failure still impacts all users in that region.

**2. Tenant cells** — Each cell serves a subset of tenants. Tenants are assigned to cells via consistent hashing. A cell failure impacts only those tenants.

**3. Shard cells** — Each cell serves a subset of data (e.g., user_id hash range). Used for database-heavy workloads where each cell owns a data partition.

**Hybrid:** Most production CBA implementations use a hybrid — regional cells containing tenant cells. User requests go to the nearest region, then to the correct tenant cell within that region.

sections.title: Cell-Based Routing — The Super-Router Pattern
sections.code:
-- Super-router architecture for cell-based routing --

1. Client Request
   |
   v
2. Global Load Balancer (Route53 / CloudFront / GCLB)
   |  -> Route to nearest region based on latency
   v
3. Regional Load Balancer (ALB / NLB)
   |  -> Strip TLS, forward to Super-Router
   v
4. Super-Router (stateless, horizontally scalable)
   |  Input: tenant_id (from JWT, header, or path)
   |  Output: cell URL (cell-3.us-east-1.internal.example.com)
   |
   |  Logic:
   |  cell_id = hash(tenant_id) % NUM_CELLS
   |  cell_url = cell_catalog[cell_id].internal_url
   |
   |  On failure (cell returns 5xx):
   |  cell_id = (cell_id + 1) % NUM_CELLS  (spillover)
   |  Retry with exponential backoff, max 3 retries
   |
   v
5. Cell Load Balancer
   |  -> Route to cell's internal services
   v
6. Cell Services (complete stack within the cell)
   |  -> API gateway    |-> Auth service
   |  -> Business logic |-> Payment service
   |  -> Database       |-> Cache (ElastiCache)
   |  -> Queue (SQS)    |-> Workers
   |
   v
7. Response back through the chain

sections.title: Cell-Based Architecture — Benefits vs Tradeoffs
rows: Blast radius = 1/N. A bad deployment in one cell stays there.
Stateless super-router with health checks. Use consistent hashing for stable cell assignment.
Canary deployments: deploy to 1 cell first, observe, then roll out.
N deployments for a full rollout (instead of 1)
Deploy to N cells in parallel with gradual rollout (1 -> 5 -> 20 -> all). Automate with CI/CD.
Noisy neighbor problem contained within a cell.
Resource fragmentation (some cells under-utilized, others over-utilized)
Dynamic cell resizing (add/remove capacity per cell). Cell-level autoscaling.
Realistic isolation testing. Test a new feature on 1% of users (1 cell).
Chaos engineering: regularly kill a cell to verify isolation. Cell-level testing in staging.
Each cell is independently operational and deployable.
N times the infrastructure. Managing N Kubernetes clusters, N databases.
Infrastructure as Code (Terraform/Pulumi). Cell templates: identical cell definition, different variables.
Cross-cell data can be eventually consistent (analytics, reporting).
No cross-cell transactions. Data is partitioned by cell.
Global secondary indexes for cross-cell queries (Materialized views, async replication). SAGA for cross-cell transactions (rare).

sections.content:
## SAP Bridge — Cell-Based Architecture for SAP

### SAP BTP Multi-Tenancy

SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) uses a multi-tenant model where multiple customers share infrastructure. Currently:

- **One shared Kyma/Kubernetes cluster** per region for serverless workloads
- **One shared HANA instance** can host multiple tenants (MDC — Multi-Container Database)
- **One Cloud Foundry foundation** per region shared by all tenants

This creates a noise-neighbor problem: one tenant's heavy workload can degrade performance for all tenants.

### Cell-Based Architecture for SAP BTP

**Proposal:** Partition BTP tenants into cells:

| Aspect | Current (Shared) | Cell-Based |
|---|---|---|
| Infrastructure | One large cluster per region | Multiple small clusters (cells) per region |
| Tenant assignment | All tenants in one pool | Tenants assigned to cells via consistent hashing |
| Isolation | Soft (resource limits) | Hard (separate Kubernetes, separate HANA) |
| Blast radius | A BTP outage impacts all customers | A cell outage impacts only its tenants |
| Cost efficiency | High (shared infrastructure) | Moderate (resource fragmentation) |
| SLA | Per-region | Per-cell (1/N blast radius) |

### SAP HANA Cell Concept

SAP HANA Cloud already supports a form of cell-based architecture through **HANA Database Containers (HDC)** — each tenant gets their own HANA container within a shared HANA instance. This is cell-like at the database layer but not at the infrastructure layer.

A true cell-based HANA would deploy separate HANA instances (not just containers) per cell, with separate compute, storage, and networking. This is more expensive but provides true failure isolation.

sections.title: Cell-Based Architecture FAQ for Staff+ Interviews

tabs.label: Design a Cell
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Design a cell-based architecture for a multi-tenant SaaS platform."

**Staff+ answer:** "Three layers:

**Layer 1: Router** — Global load balancer routes to the nearest regional super-router. The super-router deterministically maps tenant_id to cell using consistent hashing (rendezvous hashing for minimal rebalancing). Caches the mapping for 5 seconds to reduce lookup latency.

**Layer 2: Cell** — Each cell is a complete deployment: Kubernetes cluster (EKS/GKE), PostgreSQL database (RDS/Aurora), cache (ElastiCache/Memorystore), queue (SQS/PubSub), and all microservices. Cells are created from a Terraform template with cell_id as the only variable.

**Layer 3: Global Control Plane** — Cell catalog (e.g., DynamoDB Global Table) that maps cell_id -> cell URL and health status. Deploy pipeline that rolls out to cells gradually (1 cell, then 5%, then 25%, then 100%). Global monitoring dashboard that shows per-cell health.

**The key design decision:** How many tenants per cell? Too few = expensive (under-utilized). Too many = large blast radius. Rule of thumb: 100-1000 tenants per cell, such that a cell outage doesn't exceed 1% blast radius."

**Why this impresses:** You gave a three-layer design with specific technologies (consistent hashing, Terraform, DynamoDB Global Table) and a concrete sizing rule (100-1000 tenants, 1% blast radius).

tabs.label: Cell Migration
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "How do you migrate from a shared infrastructure to cells without downtime?"

**Staff+ answer:** "Four-phase migration:

**Phase 1: Prepare** — Make the application stateless (externalize sessions, make idempotent). Add tenant_id to every request. Build cell templates (Terraform). Create the super-router.

**Phase 2: Parallel Cells** — Deploy Cell 1 alongside the existing shared infrastructure. Route 1% of tenants to Cell 1 via the super-router. Validate that Cell 1 works correctly. This is a dark launch: the old infrastructure still processes all requests, Cell 1 is canary.

**Phase 3: Incremental Migration** — Gradually increase the percentage of tenants routed to cells (5%, 25%, 50%, 100%). At each step, validate service health, error rates, and latency. If issues arise, route tenants back to the shared infrastructure.

**Phase 4: Decommission** — Once all tenants are on cells, decommission the shared infrastructure. The super-router now routes all traffic to cells.

**The hardest part:** Data migration. Existing data may not be cleanly partitioned by tenant_id. You need a data re-partitioning job that runs in the background while Phase 2/3 progresses."

**Why this impresses:** You gave a concrete 4-phase migration with a dark launch pattern, percentage-based rollout, and highlighted the hardest part (data migration).

tabs.label: SAP Scenario
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "How would you make SAP BTP cell-based?"

**Staff+ answer:** "BTP's biggest risk is the multi-tenant Kyma cluster. If one tenant's workload causes a cluster-wide issue (CNI failure, etcd overload), all BTP customers are impacted. Cell-based architecture mitigates this.

**Design:**
- A cell = one Kyma cluster + one HANA Cloud instance + one event mesh
- Each cell serves 50-100 tenants (smaller cells = smaller blast radius)
- Tenant assignment: consistent hashing on BTP subaccount ID
- Super-router: BTP's Cloud Foundry router (gorouter) extended with cell routing

**The challenge:** BTP services are deeply integrated (Kyma -> HANA -> Event Mesh -> AI Core -> Integration Suite). A cell boundary means each cell needs its own instance of each service. This is expensive.

**Compromise:** 'Soft cells' — logical isolation within shared infrastructure using Kubernetes namespaces and HANA MDC containers. Cheaper than true cells, but blast radius is larger (a Kyma cluster outage still impacts all soft cells in that cluster).

**The FAANG perspective:** Start with soft cells (1 cluster per region, limited blast radius). Graduate to hard cells (separate clusters) for high-value customers willing to pay for premium isolation."

**Why this impresses:** You gave a concrete design for BTP (+Kyma, HANA, gorouter), identified the real challenge (deep service integration), and proposed a pragmatic two-tier approach (soft cells for standard, hard cells for premium).

tabs.label: Blast Radius Tradeoff
tabs.content:
**Interviewer:** "Your CTO says '100 cells is too expensive. Why not 10?' How do you respond?"

**Staff+ answer:** "Let's quantify the tradeoff.

**10 cells** (each serves 10% of users):
- Infrastructure cost: 10x (10 complete stacks)
- Blast radius: 10% per cell failure
- Probability of at least one cell failure per month: ~99.9% (if each cell has 5% failure rate)
- Expected monthly user impact: 10% x 99.9% = 9.99% of user-months

**100 cells** (each serves 1% of users):
- Infrastructure cost: 100x (100 complete stacks) - but each cell is smaller, so actual cost is ~30-50x, not 100x
- Blast radius: 1% per cell failure
- Probability of at least one cell failure: ~99.9% (same failure rate per cell)
- Expected monthly user impact: 1% x 99.9% = 0.999% of user-months

**Cost-Benefit:** 100 cells costs 3-5x more than 10 cells. But reduces user impact by 10x (10% -> 1%).

**The decision:** For a consumer product (Google Search, Meta), 10 cells might be acceptable. For an enterprise platform (SAP, Salesforce), 100 cells is the minimum because SLAs are contractual and each % of blast radius represents millions in liability.

**Recommendation:** Start with 10 cells, measure blast radius incidents, expand to 50-100 cells as the cost of incidents exceeds the cost of additional cells."

**Why this impresses:** You didn't pick a side. You presented a data-driven framework (cost vs blast radius) with concrete numbers and let the business context determine the answer. This is Staff+ reasoning.

sections.title: Production Cell-Based Architectures
rows: Any single cell failure is invisible to most users
Consistent hashing rebalancing when cells are added
Cross-cell data replication for graph queries
Enterprise SLAs with contractually guaranteed isolation
Org migration between pods is complex and manual
City-level isolation (a failure in NYC doesn't affect SF)
Hundreds of microservices per cell, duplication overhead
Deep service integration makes hard cells costly

cards.title: Blast Radius Is the Metric
cards.body: The entire point of CBA is to reduce blast radius. Optimize for 1/N cells. Measure blast radius in every incident postmortem.

cards.title: Cells = Complete Stacks
cards.body: A cell is not just a database shard. It's a complete, self-contained deployment of everything — compute, storage, networking, monitoring.

cards.title: Super-Router Is Critical Path
cards.body: The super-router is the only shared component. It must be stateless, horizontally scalable, and have 99.999% uptime. Every millisecond of latency adds to every request.

cards.title: Canary Deployments to Cells
cards.body: Deploy to 1 cell first. Observe for 10 minutes. Roll out to 25%, then 100%. If things break, only the canary cell's tenants are affected.

cards.title: CBA Is Expensive
cards.body: N cells cost 3-50x more than shared infrastructure (economies of scale are lost). The cost is justified by blast radius reduction and SLA guarantees.

cards.title: Start Soft, Graduate to Hard
cards.body: Start with logical cells (shared infrastructure, isolated namespaces). Graduate to hard cells (separate infrastructure) for high-value workloads or when blast radius incidents become too expensive.
